pages.content:
About Nin Nin

Writer. Game Developer. Creator of Imitatia.com.

Hi there! I’m Nin Nin, also known as Nin Xyami, and I’m the mind behind Imitatia.com, a digital space where storytelling, philosophy, and personal expression come to life.

Who Am I?

I’m a game developer, writer, and lifelong computer enthusiast. I graduated from the London College of Communication, and for the past 15 years, I’ve been quietly building Imitatia, writing stories, designing game concepts, and thinking deeply about the world. Imitatia is the result of years of reflection, creativity, and persistence.

This website is more than just a portfolio. It’s a personal haven. A place to speak freely. A digital journal made public, not because I want attention, but because I believe words can connect us. Even when they come from a quiet place.

What I Write

I write with honesty and intention. Most of my posts are philosophical, existential, or deeply introspective. You’ll find thoughts on life, human nature, society, emotions, and the occasional rant about things people usually avoid talking about.

I’m not afraid to be blunt or say things that challenge the norm. I believe people appreciate raw, unfiltered truth, even when it’s uncomfortable. That’s the kind of connection I’m aiming for here. Not polished perfection, but something real.

And yes, I’m open to being wrong. I welcome feedback, disagreement, and open conversations. If something I write doesn’t sit right with you, feel free to speak up. I’m always listening.

What I Build

Aside from writing, I’m an independent narrative game developer. I’m currently working on story-rich games that focus on choice, emotion, and the human condition.

I’ve been heavily inspired by the Mass Effect series, not just the gameplay, but the emotional weight, character-driven storytelling, and player agency. My goal is to build games that leave an emotional mark, not just a high score. It’ll take time, but every line of code and every sentence written is a step toward that dream.

Why Imitatia Exists

Imitatia is a place for ideas that don’t fit anywhere else. It’s for creative writing, blogs that question the world, and projects that take time. Some of it is fictional, some of it is personal, but all of it is true to who I am.

Whether you’re here to read a novel, follow a game in progress, or dive into a philosophical blog post, I hope you find something that resonates.

Let’s Talk

If you’re someone who values meaning over noise, stories over trends, and truth over filters, welcome. This space was made for you too.

Feel free to reach out, leave a comment, or just explore at your own pace. Imitatia is still growing, and I’m glad you’re here at the start.

-Nin Nin

An upcoming puzzle-driven heist adventure

Overview

Point and Touch Thief Project is a puzzle-based heist game with 8 unique scenarios. No combat, no quick reflexes—just clever planning and problem-solving as you figure out the perfect way to pull off each theft. You are a skilled thief—careful, precise, and always one step ahead. Each job is a new challenge: a different location, new obstacles, and unique tricks to get past security. But every heist has consequences, and sooner or later, your luck might run out…

pages.title: Prison Sandbox
pages.excerpt: Tagline: Every choice shapes your sentence. Overview: Prison Sandbox is an open-ended survival and escape simulator that puts you inside the walls of a fully simulated prison. Here, every inmate has a story, every guard has a routine, and every rumor can change your fate. You’ll live the day-to-day
pages.content:
Tagline:
Every choice shapes your sentence.

Overview:
Prison Sandbox is an open-ended survival and escape simulator that puts you inside the walls of a fully simulated prison. Here, every inmate has a story, every guard has a routine, and every rumor can change your fate.

You’ll live the day-to-day grind of prison life — from roll call to lockdown — while navigating shifting alliances, an underground economy, and unpredictable events. The prison reacts to your actions: guards remember your offenses, factions respond to your loyalty or betrayal, and the flow of contraband changes with every shake-up.

Your goal? That’s up to you. Plan a bold escape through tunnels or disguises, manipulate your way into an early release, incite chaos to mask your getaway, or rise through the ranks to control the prison from the inside. With multiple systems intertwining — from gossip and bribery to riots and long-term heist-like plans — no two runs will ever play out the same.

The World

The prison isn’t just a backdrop — it’s alive.
Every inmate has a personality, every guard follows a routine, and the balance of power is always shifting. Corridors echo with deals whispered under breath, fights spark without warning in the yard, and rumors travel faster than contraband.

The facility itself is a maze of cell blocks, workshops, kitchens, medical bays, and hidden corners where the right player can find opportunity. Day and night cycles bring different risks: daylight means watchful guards and rigid schedules, while night opens the door to stealth — and danger.

Your world is ruled by two forces: the official system of rules and routines, and the unofficial network of favors, debts, and threats that runs beneath it. Both are dangerous. Both can be used to your advantage.

Life Inside

Daily Routine
Your days are structured around roll calls, meals, work shifts, recreation, and lockdowns. Missing any of them draws attention — unless you know how to create the right distractions. Every schedule is an obstacle and an opportunity.

Factions & Alliances
Prison life runs on loyalty and reputation. You’ll meet gang leaders, loners, informants, and opportunists. Choosing sides can gain you protection and resources, but burn too many bridges and you’ll find yourself alone in a place where no one survives alone.

The Underground Economy
Money isn’t just cash here — cigarettes, snacks, stolen tools, and even information can be traded. You can work legitimate jobs like laundry or kitchen duty for a slow trickle of resources, or take bigger risks by smuggling goods, running scams, or manipulating others into doing your dirty work.

Gossip & Rumors
Stories spread fast inside the walls. Some are true, others are traps. Your name — and what people think you’ve done — will open doors or slam them shut.

Constant Tension
Violence can erupt at any moment, lockdowns can disrupt your plans, and cell searches can wipe out weeks of preparation. Survival means adapting, watching, and always having a backup plan.

Power & Survival

Social Manipulation
Inside these walls, strength isn’t just about muscle — it’s about leverage. You can earn influence through favors, intimidation, or calculated generosity. Protect a weaker inmate and you might gain a loyal ally. Betray the wrong person, and you could have a price on your head before nightfall.

Guard Dynamics
Guards aren’t just faceless obstacles. Each has routines, preferences, and a tolerance level for trouble. Some are by-the-book and hard to sway. Others can be bribed, blackmailed, or simply distracted. The more heat you draw, the more attention they give you — and not the good kind.

Fear & Respect
Your reputation is currency. A feared inmate can command silence and obedience, while a respected one can unite allies for bigger plans. How you earn that standing — through intimidation, kindness, or cunning — is entirely up to you.

Conflict & Retaliation
Fights are a fact of life. Sometimes you’ll want to start them; other times you’ll be avoiding them at all costs. A brawl in the yard might be a personal vendetta… or a cover for slipping away unnoticed.

The Long Game
Power isn’t won in a day. Building influence, smuggling the right tools, and manipulating events take time. Patience and foresight are as valuable as any weapon.

Breaking Out

There’s no single “right” way to escape. Every prison run is its own puzzle, and the pieces change depending on your choices, relationships, and timing.

Physical Escapes

  • Tunneling: Dig under walls over weeks, hiding dirt and covering your tracks.
  • Climbing: Use stolen tools or improvised gear to scale fences and walls.
  • Breaking Locks: Craft makeshift lockpicks or bribe access to restricted keys.
  • Disguises: Blend into work crews or pose as a guard to slip out unnoticed.

Social Engineering

  • Forged Papers: Manipulate bureaucracy and fake early release forms.
  • Bribery: Pay the right person at the right time to look the other way.
  • Setups: Frame others to shift suspicion away from yourself.

Chaos Routes

  • Riots: Stir up unrest to distract guards and open escape paths.
  • Mass Escapes: Turn the prison into a stampede of bodies and confusion.
  • Targeted Disruptions: Cause “accidents” that disable key security systems.

Multi-Step Plans
Some escapes require days or weeks of careful preparation — stealing tools, gathering allies, studying patrols, and staging diversions. Success means freedom; failure might mean solitary, transfer, or starting over in worse conditions.

Replayability

Every run of Prison Sandbox unfolds differently.

  • Procedural Events: Random cell searches, guard shifts, contraband shortages, and unexpected opportunities keep you on your toes.
  • Dynamic Relationships: NPC attitudes shift based on your choices, meaning the same plan may fail — or succeed — in a new run.
  • Evolving Economy: Prices and scarcity change based on your actions and the prison’s state.
  • Multiple Endings: Escape, dominate the prison, live quietly in the shadows, or fail spectacularly.

Connected Universe

For those who’ve played Point & Touch Thief, there’s an extra twist — the thief protagonist will appear in Prison Sandbox as a special inmate with unique dialogue, hidden connections, and exclusive opportunities.
This cameo not only rewards returning players but also adds an extra layer of replayability, letting you experience certain events and escape routes in ways no other character can.

Status & Development

Current Stage: Early concept and design phase.
Planned Release: TBD.
Platforms: PC (initial release), with potential console ports later.
Genre: Sandbox / Strategy / Simulation.
Tone: Semi-dark, gritty, and player-driven.

We’re starting small with a playable prototype before expanding into the full vision — a living, reactive prison where every choice matters and every run tells a different story

Dev Blog

Follow the journey of Prison Sandbox from concept to completion.
Our development blog shares behind-the-scenes updates, design insights, and progress milestones — along with the occasional sneak peek at art, mechanics, and in-game events.

Check out the latest posts here: Prison Sandbox Dev Blog →

pages.excerpt: Read Now By Nin Nin & KillSwitch Spoiler free series page Tagline: A crowded party, a restless night, and a stranger’s quiet gaze, the veil between ordinary and unknown begins to shift. Overview The Infection is a character driven sci fi horror about a mother who will cross a sealed neighborhood to
pages.content:
Read Now

By Nin Nin & KillSwitch
Spoiler free series page

Tagline: A crowded party, a restless night, and a stranger’s quiet gaze, the veil between ordinary and unknown begins to shift.

Overview

The Infection is a character driven sci fi horror about a mother who will cross a sealed neighborhood to reach her daughter after green light splits the sky and reality changes. Inside the barrier, nights glow, rules bend, and kindness can be the difference between turning and surviving. This is Angela’s story of courage, fear, and the cost of protecting the people you love.

  • Genre: Sci Fi Horror, Survival Thriller, Mystery
  • Status: Part One complete draft, revisions in progress
  • Length: Novel, multi part arc
  • Content notes: Survival peril, creature attacks, state violence, grief

Setting

The story unfolds inside the Barrier, a shimmering dome that seals off suburban streets from the outside world. Within it, the sky glows an unnatural green, time feels off, and devices sometimes behave like they are listening. Houses become fortresses, supermarkets become waypoints, and familiar places turn strange. Outside the barrier there is only black emptiness, or so it seems from within.

Tone & Style

Intimate, tense, and cinematic. The voice stays close to Angela, focusing on small human details in the middle of chaos: a hand on a doorknob that will not turn, the weight of a phone with no signal, the way a quiet girl watches the sky. Dialogue is natural and clipped when fear rises. Action is clear and grounded. The story favors atmosphere over gore and lets dread build in layers.

Themes (no spoilers)

  • Motherhood and resolve: how love narrows choices and sharpens courage.
  • Kindness under pressure: small mercies that echo through disaster.
  • Trust and authority: rescue can look like control, safety can have a price.
  • Identity under change: what remains when rules, bodies, and neighbors do not stay the same.
  • Hope as strategy: holding on is not passive, it is a plan.

Status

Part One outlines and chapters are complete through Angela’s capture and the outside push for a cure. Current work focuses on polish, continuity, and preparing the series page for weekly releases. Cover and concept art drafts are ready. Chapter links will appear here as they go live.

Author’s Note

This story began with a single image: a house full of music, a sudden hush, and green light on glass. From there it became a journey about a mother and a city that refuses to stay ordinary. We wrote it to feel human first, terrifying second. Thank you for reading, for sharing, and for believing that kindness still matters when the lights go out.
— Nin Nin & KillSwitch

About the Authors

KillSwitch is the main storyteller behind The Infection. She specializes in horror and mystery, crafting unsettling worlds and unforgettable characters. Her ability to blend suspense with emotion makes her one of the most compelling storytellers writing today.
Nin Nin serves as the editor and co-writer, shaping the narrative, tightening the flow, and polishing the work for readers while preserving the raw intensity of KillSwitch’s vision.

Overview

Under the Cherry Blossoms follows Lily Andrews, a fresh face in the bustling world of Los Angeles, as she steps into her first job at Mystique Holdings. What begins as a simple internship soon turns into something more, as she finds herself unexpectedly drawn to her boss—Ethan Harris, a man marked by a past he can’t seem to let go of.

As their relationship deepens, so do the emotional scars they try to hide. Ethan’s past love continues to haunt him, while Lily's unwavering warmth begins to guide him toward healing.

But the calm is disrupted by the presence of a mysterious pink-haired woman, whose quiet influence lingers in the background—complex, intriguing, and perhaps not entirely benign.

pages.excerpt: What if stealth wasn’t about silenced pistols and disguises, but claws, fangs, and shadows in the trees? Untamed Shadows is a stealth sandbox that asks this exact question. Instead of playing a suited assassin with gadgets, you step into the role of a tigress — a mother, a hunter, and a strategist —
pages.content:
What if stealth wasn’t about silenced pistols and disguises, but claws, fangs, and shadows in the trees? Untamed Shadows is a stealth sandbox that asks this exact question. Instead of playing a suited assassin with gadgets, you step into the role of a tigress — a mother, a hunter, and a strategist — navigating a living, breathing jungle where every faction, predator, and prey matters.

This is not a nature documentary. It’s not a survival sim where you grind for food. It’s a Hitman-inspired stealth experience, reimagined through the lens of an apex predator. Guns, gadgets, and disguises are replaced with animal abilities, ecosystem tricks, and raw instinct. The core fantasy is simple yet powerful: be the shadow in the jungle, rescue your cub, and prove you are the deadliest force alive.

Core vision

At its heart, Untamed Shadows is a 3D stealth sandbox. You stalk, you plan, you set up “accidents,” you strike — and then vanish into the foliage.

Genre and fantasy: A 3D stealth sandbox where you are a tigress, a lone infiltrator, a mother on a rescue.

Core loop: Scout → set up animal-powered tools → isolate targets → execute cleanly → disappear.

Pillars

  • Stealth comes first. Fighting is what you do when things go wrong.
  • Factions have politics. Alliances unlock tools, grudges close doors.
  • Creative kills define the sandbox. Environmental accidents, distractions, long-range eliminations, never a human projectile in your paws.
  • Believability beats strict realism. Rules stay consistent and fun takes priority over a nature documentary.

World and factions

A living forest surrounds a human village. Territories overlap, grudges simmer, and every biome changes how you hunt.

Tigers — Neutral guardians
Solitary apex predators who refuse to fight humans as policy. Proud and territorial. They respect strength and will defend borders, but they will not join your rescue at the start because they believe it is suicide.

Lions — Rival apex
A valley pride that is political, organized, and very sure of its crown. You face the Lion Leader in a mandatory duel. You defeat him, you spare him to avoid a war, and you leave with information that matters.

Wolves — The cunning upstarts
A pack that used to be pushed around, until the Black Wolf appeared. He is a lab-enhanced, direwolf-like alpha who forged them into a force. Wolves are the only animals known to infiltrate human villages.

Pumas — The displaced heirs
Masters of stealth and surgical ambush who once ruled the mountains. The wolves drove them down to lakes after the rise of Black Wolf. Proud, bitter, and looking for a way to regain dignity.

Humans — The wild card
Hunters, guards, villagers, jeeps, dogs, outposts, traps. Guns are lethal. One or two shots end your run. The One-Eyed Hunter leads them. He is the scarred antagonist who kidnapped your cub. Five hunter mini-bosses sit between you and him, each with multiple sandbox kill routes.

Elephants — Neutral kings
Herds that dominate space by simply existing. You do not fight them. You reroute around them. They set boundaries for everyone.

Other wildlife
Bears as rare mini-boss hazards. Crocodiles that turn rivers into traps. Monkeys and birds that add noise, mischief, and systems to the world.

Keystone leaders and faces

  • The Tigress, the player: Former de-facto tiger leader, wounded and dethroned, now committed to a solitary rescue. Smaller than male apexes but terrifying through skill and ferocity. Respected after Act I, yet she refuses the crown.
  • Lion Leader: Regal rival. You defeat him in a duel, spare him, gain key intel about the cub and about wolves, then get banished from lion lands.
  • Black Wolf: A singular outlier with a body class on the level of lion or tiger. Born in a human lab, united the wolves, and drove the pumas from the mountains. Appears in one mythic mission.
  • Puma Matriarch: The strongest and oldest puma. A face of lost glory and hard pride. The counterweight to Black Wolf’s legend.
  • White Tiger: A mythic Easter egg that offers spiritual flavor rather than new abilities.

Strength hierarchy and combat balance

  • Apex tier: Lions and tigers trade wins based on context and terrain.
  • The player: You win most one-on-one fights, even against leaders in formal duels. Packs overwhelm you. Stealth remains optimal.
  • Secondary tier: Pumas and direwolves are comparable as individuals. Wolves lose one-on-one to pumas but packs are deadly.
  • Humans with guns: Always the hard counter and the reason you plan carefully.
  • Elephants: Outside the predator balance. Never an ability and never a target.

Groups versus solo, quick feel

  • Wolves: one or two are manageable, four or five are dangerous.
  • Pumas: one is manageable, three or more are dangerous.
  • Crocodiles and bears: optional high-risk confrontations that feel like mini-bosses.

Story structure

Prologue, the tutorial
A duel with a rogue tiger teaches ambush basics, pounce timing, and the kill or spare choice. It sets the tone. You are strong, yet the jungle does not bend easily.

Act I, Ambush and the hunters
The One-Eyed Hunter and his squad attack. You are wounded, your cub is taken, and the river carries you away. You return to the tigers, who refuse to help, and you learn another tiger has taken your place. You begin hunting five targets across different biomes:

  1. The Trapper with snares and bait. Lure him into his own trap, poison the stew, or strike at night.
  2. The Sharpshooter in a watchtower. Climb and ambush, topple the tower, or lure crocodiles.
  3. The Driver who runs a road patrol. Sabotage refuel points, set up a stampede, or stage a road accident.
  4. The Tracker who works with dogs. Mask your scent, distract with bones, ambush the handler.
  5. The Camp Leader near the village. Poison the pot, drop a tree on a tent, or slip in at midnight.

Act II, Lions and Wolves or Pumas
One hunter hides with the lions. You infiltrate, face the Lion Leader, then spare him. He reveals that only wolves know how to slip into human villages. You branch:

  • Wolves path: Confront Black Wolf. Spare him to ally with the wolves.
  • Pumas path: Kill Black Wolf to ally with the pumas.
  • Peace path: Rescue direwolf pups from the lab and broker a truce. Wolves relocate to the lab ruins and pumas regain the mountains.

Canon balance says Black Wolf is special. Regular direwolves appear only after the main story if the pups survive.

Act III, the finale
The village cannot be infiltrated without a distraction unless you chase the hardest possible ending. Your ally determines the distraction:

  • Wolf ally: howls and pack ambush.
  • Puma ally: silent rooftop strikes and shadow routes.
  • Peace path: both distract at once.
  • Legendary solo: no allies, no distraction, only skill.

You set the final ambush for the One-Eyed Hunter. It is a stealth climax that ends with a cinematic finish and a rescued cub.

Endings

  • Wolf ending: wolves become feared and dominant.
  • Puma ending: pumas reclaim their mountains while wolves weaken.
  • Peace ending: a fragile truce holds.
  • Legendary solo: no narration, only a pure personal triumph.

Post-game, direwolves
If you saved the pups, direwolves can spawn in the world, two or three at most. They feel close to pumas in tier. You can beat one in a fair fight, but small groups will test you. Black Wolf remains a unique legend.

Stealth and detection

States: Unaware, suspicious, alerted, combat, search reset.

Humans: Rely on sight and sound. Villagers panic and run. Hunters investigate. Dogs track scent.

Animals: Stronger smell and hearing, longer investigation tails than humans.

Scent and shadow disguises, the non-human kind

  • Mud or blood masks hide your scent from dogs and trackers and give small stamina benefits.
  • Herd shadowing lets you move within or behind herbivores at a distance to lower visual detection in open fields.
  • There is no literal human disguise. The Raven ability handles social stealth by hijacking attention.

Body handling

  • You can drag and hide bodies in bushes, roots, and shallow water, slowly and with the risk of tracks or blood.
  • Some routes auto-hide a kill, for example a puma pull off a ledge or a body carried away by water. Vultures may expose bodies later.

Loadout and tools: the jungle arsenal

Pre-mission loadout

  • One legendary hidden ability
  • Two normal abilities
  • Two gadgets
  • Your default companion, the regular monkey, once rescued. The monkey is not a slotted ability.

In-mission discovery
You can unlock an ability by finding its trigger, such as a side objective, a lure item, or a territory marker.

Free roam
Abilities are on cooldowns rather than limited inventory, so you can use them repeatedly with thoughtful pacing.

Normal abilities, pick two

Puma, silent precision
One isolated target, clean takedown, auto-hide into a bush, off a ledge, or into water. Refuses to fight groups. Ideal for Silent Assassin paths.

Wolves, distraction and sabotage
Howls, noise, broken equipment, spooked prey, pulled patrols. Creates chaos that can raise suspicion. No direct human kill.

Lion or lioness, brute force strike
Unlocked by rescuing a lioness and her cubs. Instantly mauls a strong human such as an armored guard. Always loud, body exposed, limited charges.

Tigers, apex versatility
Unlocked after you defeat a tiger leader in Act I. Early on it helps with predator control against animals. After Act II it gains anti-human options. Stealth maul leaves a body. Brute charge causes panic. Not as clean as the puma and uses limited charges.

Default companion: the monkey

The monkey is your pair of hands for tasks the tigress cannot do.

Core actions

  • Deliver poison to food or drinks when you bring the berries or a snake.
  • Steal keys from scripted or distracted targets.
  • Unlock doors or chests if he has a key.

Upgrades

  • Stage one, poisoning.
  • Stage two, key stealing that depends on timing and distraction.
  • Stage three, primitive picklock if you collect special wood stems in free roam. It is slow, risky, and works on limited locks.

The monkey cannot perform creative accidents or use complex machines. That specialty belongs to the lab chimp legendary ability.

Poison berries

  • Red for lethal with a delay, ideal for an isolated collapse.
  • Blue for sleep, a non-lethal path.
  • Purple for emetic and panic, perfect for pulling a target away.
  • Black for a delayed toxin that is hard to trace.

Flow: identify the food, distract with wolves or roars, send the monkey, wait for the delayed outcome. Crowded areas raise the chance of the monkey getting spotted.

Legendary hidden abilities, pick one

These are big, identity-defining tools that mirror sniper, accident, disguise, and gas archetypes in an animal-authentic way. Unlike normal abilities, they are not available by default. The tigress must first discover special escaped lab animals in free roam. Finding one unlocks a dedicated side mission, and completing that mission bonds the animal to her and makes the ability usable in story contracts.

Leopard, long-range elimination
Opens a scope overlay to mark a distant target. The camera snaps to the strike. The body is obvious and messy. Suspicion spikes and NPCs search the vector of origin, including your last known mark. Three to four uses per mission, long cooldown.

Lab monkey, the chimpanzee accident specialist
Pulls levers, drops crates, flips generators, releases traps, fires mounted weapons. Kills register as accidents and do not implicate the tigress. Limited uses with a long cooldown. Mischievous personality that adds dark humor without introducing random failures.

Raven, social stealth and blindness
A convincing mimic that entrances one NPC at a time. Awareness drops near zero for a short window and you can cross their line of sight. Combine with a phone distraction by having the monkey dial a camp phone. Long cooldown and nearby NPCs may get suspicious.

Skunk, crowd control and area denial
Spray a location. Humans gag and evacuate, abandoning posts. This is non-lethal space making. One or two uses with a medium cooldown.

White Tiger
A mythic Easter egg encounter, not a gameplay ability.

Gadgets, one-use tools

Poison berries, mini snake, scorpion for bedroll stings, beehive for panic, log or branch for accidents, bone for dog distraction, fish or meat for healing and bait, mud for scent disguise and stamina. You find these in levels and bring two in your loadout.

Ability unlocks and in-mission discovery

  • Lioness call: rescue a lioness and cubs inside the mission.
  • Wolves: leave a steak at a den or a marker to gain a charge.
  • Pumas: set a deer carcass or a perfect cliff perch to enable a call.
  • Tigers: find fresh scratch marks or a blood trail in tiger territory.

Free roam uses cooldowns. Missions consume charges. No spamming.

Faction alignment and branching

There is no visible respect meter. The world shows how it feels about you through missions and attitudes.

  • Help Black Wolf for full wolf alliance and permanent puma hostility.
  • Help the Puma Matriarch for full puma alliance and permanent wolf hostility.
  • Rescue direwolf pups for a truce where both sides assist.
  • Walk the neutral or lone path for harder runs with fewer tools and more purity.

In free roam, tolerated factions offer gigs. Hostile factions refuse or attack. The voices feel distinct: wolves boast, pumas grumble with pride, lions snark, the tigress stays cool and stoic.

Side missions

Predator-coded tasks that reinforce identity. You do not play bodyguard for herbivores.

  • For wolves: steal puma caches, spoil meals, stage petty humiliations, run stealth races against puma timers.
  • For pumas: silent hunts, perfect cache placements, avoid wolf detection.
  • For lions: tribute hunts that test your willingness to show deference.
  • Smaller animals: monkeys that steal camp loot, birds that reveal patrol routes.
  • Hunts: deer, boar, or fish for goodwill with predators.

Mission rhythm

  1. Recon: track routes and scents, mark opportunities.
  2. Setup: choose berry type, plant a beehive or a log, place a steak, find tiger scratches, identify phones.
  3. Isolate: pull guards with wolves, use Raven plus a phone to blind a sentinel, send the monkey for poison or a key.
  4. Execute: puma for a clean kill, lab chimp for an accident, leopard for a distant mark.
  5. Exit: drag or hide the body if needed, clear a corridor with skunk, switch routes as suspicion rises.

UI and UX notes

  • Ability wheel: one legendary in slot A, two normals in slots B and C, monkey actions, and gadgets.
  • Leopard scope: recognizable crosshair with an animal twist and clear investigation vectors after the shot.
  • Monkey command wheel: poison, key steal for scripted states, unlocks, simple levers.
  • Alerts: distinct human versus animal cues, scent indicators for dogs, visible panic zones for the skunk.
  • Clarity first: tooltips always show effect and risk, for example, Tiger Stealth kills silently but leaves a body.

Tone, humor, and atmosphere

Primal stealth thriller is the spine. Rivalries add flavor and relief.

  • Wolves gloat when they steal food.
  • Pumas flick tails and growl in dry annoyance.
  • Lions deliver theatrical disdain.
  • The monkey fist-pumps on success and chitters if spotted.

Awe moments matter: a distant elephant trumpet, a trail of broken trees, a herd crossing that redraws your route in real time.

Canon versus sandbox

The world allows non-canon actions such as fighting leaders or going on a rampage. The narrative assumes you spared the Lion Leader, followed your chosen alliance path, and executed stealth-first rescues. Canon determines who helps you in Act III and how the epilogue reads.

Quick reference, the arsenal at a glance

Legendary, pick one:
Leopard for long-range marks with a cooldown.
Lab chimp for creative accidents that look like fate.
Raven for single-target social blindness.
Skunk for short-term area denial.

Normal, pick two:
Puma for clean auto-hide kills.
Wolves for noise and sabotage.
Lion or lioness for a loud panic strike.
Tigers for predator control early and anti-human options later.

Gadgets, pick two:
Berries in red, blue, purple, and black, mini snake, scorpion, beehive, log or branch, bone, fish or meat, mud.

Default companion:
Monkey for poison delivery, key steals on scripted targets, unlocks with keys, and primitive picklocks with a special wood stem.

Why it works

  • The stealth sandbox DNA is strong, yet every tool is an animal behavior or an ecosystem trick.
  • Factions add stakes and personality. Your toolkit depends on who respects you and who wants you dead.
  • The tigress feels legendary without breaking the world. Guns, elephants, and packs still demand respect.
  • Replayability stays high through loadout choices, mid-mission unlocks, branching alliances, creative accidents, and legendary swaps.

Untamed Shadows is a love letter to stealth and to the idea that nature can be the smartest weapon in the room. If you plan like a hunter and move like a shadow, the jungle becomes your conspirator.
